question: According to the USGS, in what year was the American Alligator first found west of Texas (not including Texas)?

research_queries: USGS American Alligator distribution west of Texas first recorded
American Alligator historical range expansion USGS
USGS alligator sightings west Texas first documented year
American Alligator California Arizona New Mexico first found USGS
USGS wildlife survey American Alligator western states historical
working_plan: Search USGS databases and publications for American Alligator distribution records
Look for historical surveys or wildlife reports mentioning first western sightings
Focus on states immediately west of Texas (New Mexico, Arizona, California, etc.)
Verify the specific year from authoritative USGS sources
Cross-check multiple sources to ensure accuracy of the first documented occurrence

solver.reasoning_summary: According to the USGS Nonindigenous Aquatic Species database, the American Alligator was first documented west of Texas in 1954 in California. The database shows California as having the earliest first observation date (1954) among all states west of Texas, with specimen ID 156000 documenting an alligator found in the Colorado River. Other western states have later first records: Colorado (1978), Arizona (1990), and Nevada (2009).

Annotator Metadata.Steps:
1. Search the web for “American Alligator USGS”.
2. Click result for the USGS Species Profile.
3. Click “Animated Map”.
4. Click the “Skip years with no recorded sightings” button.
5. Zoom out on the map to better view the whole U.S.
6. Move the slider back to the beginning, then advance it until I see a red dot pop up west of Texas.
7. Note the year that the dot appears, 1954.
Annotator Metadata.Tools:
1. Search engine
2. Web browser
3. Image recognition
